metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete rate-limit policy for unknown-kid refetch in the Security section and mirror it in AC7 (e.g., 'at most 1 JWKS refetch per 30 s for unrecognized kids; test asserts ≤ 2 fetches for a flood of 50 random-kid requests').
findings.finding: The rate-limit/cooldown value for unknown-kid flood defense is unspecified — the plan says 'rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation' and AC7 says 'unbounded fetches' must not occur, but gives no concrete threshold (e.g., max 1 refetch per 30 s, or at most N total refetches for M distinct kids). The executor must choose a number, and the test assertion cannot be written as a falsifiable inequality without one.
findings.location: Security section ('rate-limited/cooled-down'); AC7 ('unknown-kid refetch is rate-limited … observable oracle via request count')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'Unknown-kid refetch is cooled down to at most 1 refetch per 30 s (configurable via `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown`, default 30).' Update AC7: 'assert the JWKS fake receives ≤ 2 GET requests when the verifier is called 50 times in rapid succession with random kids (oracle: `fake.request_count <= 2`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `JWKSTokenVerifier` must wrap `PyJWKClient`'s unknown-kid refetch with a custom cooldown (e.g., track last-refetch timestamp; suppress further refetches within a configurable window such as 30 s) since `PyJWKClient` provides no such mechanism natively — this is prerequisite for AC7's observable oracle to be achievable.
findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` does not natively rate-limit or cool-down refetches for unknown `kid` values. The plan asserts 'refetch on unknown `kid` is rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation' as a property of the chosen approach, but `PyJWKClient` unconditionally issues a new HTTP fetch for every unrecognized `kid` (it has a `lifespan` TTL for cached keys, but no cooldown or request-count cap on unknown-kid misses). Meeting AC7's observable oracle ('a flood of random `kid`s does not cause unbounded fetches — observable via the stateful JWKS fake's request count') requires custom wrapper logic the plan does not specify.
findings.location: Security section ('Key rotation without DoS') / AC line 7
findings.suggested_fix: Add an implementation note in the Approach section: 'Because PyJWKClient does not rate-limit unknown-kid refetches, JWKSTokenVerifier must maintain a `_last_refetch: float` timestamp and suppress any refetch triggered by an unknown kid within a configurable `_kid_refetch_cooldown` (default 30 s). The existing `lifespan` TTL controls legitimate cache expiry; the cooldown controls adversarial kid-flood suppression. AC7's fake request-count oracle validates this wrapper directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete rate-limit policy for unknown-kid refetch in the Security section (e.g., 'at most 1 JWKS refetch per 30 s regardless of how many distinct unknown kids arrive') and mirror the exact numbers in AC7's observable oracle (e.g., 'assert ≤ 2 total JWKS fetches for a flood of 50 random-kid requests').
findings.finding: The rate-limit policy for unknown-`kid` JWKS refetch has no concrete bound stated anywhere in the plan. AC7 asserts 'unbounded fetches do not occur' with 'observable oracle via request count,' but without a numeric threshold (e.g., 'at most 1 refetch per 30 s' or 'max 2 fetches for a flood of 50 requests') the oracle is not falsifiable — the test writer must invent the threshold and any non-infinite count satisfies 'not unbounded.'
findings.location: Security section ('rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation'); AC7 ('unknown-kid refetch is rate-limited … observable oracle via the stateful JWKS fake's request count')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, replace 'rate-limited/cooled-down' with: 'at most 1 JWKS refetch per 30 s for unrecognized kids (cooldown timer resets on a successful rotation refetch); a flood of N distinct unknown kids in < 30 s triggers exactly 1 refetch.' In AC7, change the oracle to: 'assert the fake received ≤ 2 total requests (1 initial load + 1 rotation refetch) for a flood of 50 random-kid tokens within a 1 s window.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the accepted `typ` value vocabulary for the access-token class check (e.g. `at+JWT` per RFC 9068, and/or `JWT`, with case-sensitivity rule), co-located in the What or Security section, so AC4's negative-control test has an unambiguous oracle.
findings.finding: The `typ` claim check (RFC 8725 §3.11) is mandated in both the What section and AC4 ('a wrong token class (`typ`)'), but the plan never defines the accepted `typ` value vocabulary — i.e., which string(s) constitute a valid access-token `typ` (e.g. `at+JWT`, `JWT`, `Bearer`, case-sensitivity rules). An implementer must invent this set, and a test author must pick a value to use as the oracle for AC4's 'wrong token class' negative control.
findings.location: What section ('validates…the token class (`typ`)') and AC4 ('a wrong token class (`typ`)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The `typ` check accepts `at+JWT` (RFC 9068 access token) and rejects all other values including `JWT` and `id_token`; comparison is case-insensitive per RFC 8725.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the kid-flood cooldown parameters (e.g. max N refetches per M seconds per verifier instance), state placement (per-instance), and reset semantics so AC7's 'request count' oracle is deterministic and implementers make a consistent choice.
findings.finding: The kid-flood rate-limit/cooldown state is referenced in Security and mandated as a test oracle in AC7, but the plan never specifies the numeric parameters (max refetches per window, window duration), the state's placement (per-instance vs. module-level), or its reset semantics. PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` has no built-in kid-flood protection, so the implementer must build custom counter state — yet its placement and thresholds are left entirely unspecified.
findings.location: Security section ('refetch on unknown `kid` is rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation') and AC7 ('unknown-`kid` refetch is rate-limited… observable oracle via the stateful JWKS fake's request count')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the PyJWT implementation pattern for `typ` enforcement — e.g., inspect `jwt.get_unverified_header(token)['typ']` before calling `decode()`, or check the decoded payload's `typ` claim post-decode — so the developer has a concrete mechanism and the negative-control AC is achievable.
findings.finding: The plan mandates `typ`/token-class validation (RFC 8725 §3.11) but `jwt.decode()` does not enforce the `typ` header claim by default in PyJWT, and the plan provides no implementation direction for this check. The developer must either inspect the unverified header pre-decode or use a post-decode verified-claims check, both of which are non-obvious PyJWT usage patterns.
findings.location: Security section ('a `typ`/token-class check (RFC 8725 §3.11)') and AC line 4 ('a wrong token class (`typ`)' as a negative control)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Because PyJWT does not validate the `typ` header claim during `decode()`, the verifier must explicitly check it: call `jwt.get_unverified_header(token)` and assert `typ` is in the set of accepted access-token type values (e.g., `{"at+JWT", "JWT"}` — or a configurable `auth_jwt_token_type` with default `at+JWT`) before passing the token to `decode()`. Reject tokens with a missing or disallowed `typ` by returning `None`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Approach that `PyJWKClient`'s built-in `lifespan` TTL alone does NOT throttle per-unknown-kid refetches, and that the implementer must add explicit custom rate-limiting/cooldown logic (e.g., a token bucket or last-seen-kid timestamp gate) to satisfy the AC7 flood-defense requirement.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `PyJWKClient` provides built-in 'rate-limited/cooled-down' unknown-kid refetch flood protection ('refetch on unknown kid is rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation'), but PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` only has a `lifespan` cache TTL parameter — it does NOT natively throttle or count per-unknown-kid requests. Custom rate-limiting/cooldown logic must be authored by the implementer, but the plan's Approach section presents this as a library-provided property, which may mislead the implementer into skipping the custom guard.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'PyJWKClient (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + kid lookup + refetch-on-rotation)'; Security — 'refetch on unknown kid is rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation'; AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to Approach: 'Note: `PyJWKClient`'s `lifespan` TTL prevents re-fetching for known keys but does NOT rate-limit requests triggered by unknown `kid` values. The verifier must maintain a separate per-endpoint cooldown (e.g., minimum interval between successive refetches, regardless of `kid`) to bound outbound JWKS fetches under a kid-flood attack.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts' with a definitive posture declaration (e.g., 'hard-block: verifier refuses to start if `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` resolves to a private/link-local range, raising a descriptive error') so the outbound JWKS path has an unambiguous access posture.
findings.finding: The plan's 'warn/optionally reject' language for private/link-local JWKS hosts leaves an ambiguous (undeclared) access posture on the outbound JWKS boundary path. A deployment whose operator misconfigures `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` to a cloud metadata endpoint (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/...`) may receive only a warning rather than a hard block, leaving the SSRF vector exploitable.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: … warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts (e.g. 169.254.169.254)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Security section to: 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: `jwks_uri` fixed at construction from trusted config (never the token), HTTPS-only (hard reject `http://` at startup), bounded timeout (e.g. 5 s), and refuse to start if the URI host is a private/link-local address (RFC 1918, 169.254.0.0/16, ::1, etc.) — configurable opt-out only for development with an explicit warning.' Then add a corresponding AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve 'optionally reject' to a concrete decision rule for private/link-local `jwks_uri` hosts: specify whether it is config-controlled (e.g. `auth_jwt_allow_insecure_jwks_uri=true`), always a startup error, or always a warning — and add an AC if rejection is the default.
findings.finding: The SSRF guard for private/link-local JWKS URIs uses the word 'optionally reject' in the Security section, leaving the fallback behavior undefined: it is ambiguous whether a private-host `jwks_uri` is (a) a hard startup error, (b) a per-request error that raises, or (c) only a warning that proceeds. No AC covers this path, so neither the implementation nor the test author has a grounded decision rule.
findings.location: Security section ('warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located processing protocol for `scope`/`scp` → scopes: specify precedence (e.g. `scp` array wins if present, else split `scope` by whitespace), the split delimiter, and the behavior when neither claim is present.
findings.finding: The `scope`/`scp` → `AccessToken.scopes` mapping rule is stated in the What section but the processing protocol is never fully specified: (1) which claim takes precedence when both `scope` (space-separated string) and `scp` (array) are present; (2) how a `scope` string is split (whitespace only, or also comma); (3) whether an absent scope claim produces an empty set or is a rejection. These rules are not co-located with the `AccessToken` schema that needs them.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the contradiction between 'the SDK pin already lives in S1' (Compatibility) and S3's own `file_impact` entry `pyproject.toml: 'pin mcp>=1.28.1,<2; add pyjwt[crypto]'`: either remove `pyproject.toml` from S3's file_impact (if S1 fully owns the pin) or update the Compatibility text to say 'S3 adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to the `[mcp]` extra; the SDK version pin was established in S1'.
findings.finding: The Compatibility section states 'The `pyjwt[crypto]` add is backward-compatible; the SDK pin already lives in S1' — asserting S3 does NOT need to pin `mcp`. However S3's own `file_impact` entry lists `pyproject.toml: 'pin mcp>=1.28.1,<2; add pyjwt[crypto]'`, directly contradicting this exclusion. The current `pyproject.toml` has `mcp>=1.9` (no upper bound). One of two things is true: either S1 owns the pin (and S3's file_impact is wrong/duplicative), or S3 also pins it (and the 'already lives in S1' claim is incorrect/misleading). This contradiction could cause S3's implementor to either skip pinning (trusting S1 will do it) or double-pin (trusting the file_impact), with neither source of truth clear.
findings.location: Compatibility section — 'the SDK pin already lives in S1'; file_impact entry for pyproject.toml
findings.suggested_fix: Update Compatibility to: 'The `pyjwt[crypto]` add to the `[mcp]` extra is backward-compatible. The mcp SDK version pin (`mcp>=1.28.1,<2`) was established in S1; S3 only adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to `pyproject.toml`.' Then update the file_impact reason to `"add pyjwt[crypto] to [mcp] extra"` only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC(s) covering: (a) verifier rejects (raises at construction or fetch time) a `jwks_uri` that is not HTTPS; (b) JWKS fetch uses a bounded timeout (observable via the fake's elapsed time or a mock); (c) verifier warns or refuses a `jwks_uri` resolving to a private/link-local address.
findings.finding: The Security section specifies three concrete SSRF-safety requirements — (1) HTTPS-only enforcement on `jwks_uri`, (2) bounded fetch timeout, and (3) warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts (e.g. 169.254.169.254) — but no Acceptance Criterion tests or verifies any of these properties. They are described deliverables with no observable oracle.
findings.location: Security section ('SSRF-safe JWKS fetch') vs Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC8)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one or two unit tests to the AC list: one asserting construction/startup raises (or fetch raises) when `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` uses an `http://` scheme; one asserting a configurable/hardcoded timeout is applied (mock the HTTP client); optionally one asserting a warning/error for a 169.254.x.x URI. These match the 'stateful fake' pattern already established in AC3/AC7.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the JWKS refetch (PyJWKClient or equivalent) is executed off the event loop (e.g., `asyncio.to_thread`) or replaced with an async-native JWKS client so synchronous I/O never blocks the ASGI event loop on a cache miss.
findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` uses synchronous urllib internally. Every JWKS cache miss (cold start, key rotation refetch) will issue a blocking synchronous HTTP request on the calling thread. In an async ASGI server (FastMCP is ASGI-based), this blocks the event loop for the duration of the network round-trip, stalling all other in-flight requests.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Security (SSRF-safe JWKS fetch) / AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` (or the full `verify()` call) in `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` so the blocking urllib call is dispatched to the thread pool. Alternatively, adopt `python-jose` or a custom httpx-based async JWKS fetcher, but `asyncio.to_thread` is the lowest-friction fix for the existing PyJWT dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that tests SSRF-protection rejection paths: (a) verifier raises/refuses-to-start when `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` is an `http://` URI; (b) verifier raises when the URI resolves to a known private/link-local range (e.g. `169.254.169.254`); observable oracle: startup or fetch raises with a descriptive error.
findings.finding: The plan's Security section declares SSRF-safe JWKS fetch as a hard requirement (HTTPS-only URI, rejection of private/link-local hosts such as 169.254.169.254, bounded timeout), but no Acceptance Criterion tests any of these rejection paths — a configuration with an HTTP jwks_uri or a private-range URI would silently pass the test suite.
findings.location: Security section ('SSRF-safe JWKS fetch') vs Acceptance Criteria (AC3–AC8)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'Unit test asserts the verifier refuses to start (or raises on first fetch) when `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` is an `http://` URI (HTTPS-only enforcement) AND when the host is a private/link-local address — each a distinct negative control with a descriptive exception as the observable oracle.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `JWTVerifier` exists in the installed `mcp` SDK (e.g. `python -c 'from mcp.server.fastmcp import JWTVerifier'`); if it does not, remove or correct the 'same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' rationale sentence so implementors are not sent searching for a non-existent reference.
findings.finding: The plan's Approach & rationale states 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as a rationale for using `PyJWKClient` + `jwt.decode`. `JWTVerifier` is UNRESOLVED across every probed SDK module path (`mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.auth`, `mcp.server.auth.handlers`, `mcp.server.fastmcp.security`) — no evidence this symbol exists in the installed mcp SDK. If `JWTVerifier` does not exist, the rationale rests on an unverifiable premise, and developers may lose time searching for a reference implementation that isn't there.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section — 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in Approach that `PyJWKClient` uses synchronous `urllib` and specify how JWKS fetches will be dispatched off the asyncio event loop (e.g., `loop.run_in_executor(None, client.get_signing_key_from_jwt, token)`) to avoid blocking the ASGI server on cache misses.
findings.finding: The plan's Approach commits to `jwt.PyJWKClient` for JWKS fetch without acknowledging that `PyJWKClient` uses synchronous `urllib` internally. FastMCP's streamable-HTTP transport is async (ASGI/Starlette); a JWKS cache miss (cold start, key rotation, or unknown kid) will block the asyncio event loop on the calling thread. The plan neither names this constraint nor specifies the mitigation (e.g., `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, …)` or a thread pool dispatch).
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'Use PyJWT's PyJWKClient (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache…)'; Security — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: jwks_uri fixed at construction… bounded timeout'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an implementation note specifying that `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` (or the enclosing verify call) must be dispatched off the event loop via `asyncio.to_thread(...)` (or equivalent) because `PyJWKClient` is synchronous and the FastMCP ASGI server runs on an async event loop.
findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` uses a synchronous `urllib`-based HTTP client internally. The plan describes using it on the 'S2 seam' of a FastMCP ASGI server (async event loop) without mentioning that every JWKS cache miss (cold start, key rotation refetch, or unknown-kid refetch) will issue a blocking synchronous HTTP call on the calling thread. In an ASGI server the auth middleware runs on the event loop thread, so an unshielded synchronous fetch stalls the entire event loop for the HTTPS round-trip duration.
findings.location: Approach & rationale ('Use PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + `kid` lookup + refetch-on-rotation)')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Because PyJWKClient is synchronous (urllib-based), wrap the JWKS key-fetch call in `await asyncio.to_thread(client.get_signing_key_from_jwt, token)` so the blocking HTTP round-trip is dispatched to the thread pool and never stalls the ASGI event loop.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs for SSRF-safety rejection paths on the outbound JWKS boundary: (a) verifier raises at startup or fetch when `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` is an `http://` URI; (b) JWKS fetch enforces a bounded timeout (observable via the stateful fake or a mock); (c) verifier raises/warns when the URI host is a private/link-local address — each with a falsifiable observable oracle.
findings.finding: The Security section declares three SSRF-safety properties as hard requirements (HTTPS-only URI, bounded fetch timeout, private/link-local host rejection) but no Acceptance Criterion provides an observable oracle for any of them. An implementation that omits all three would pass every AC in the plan.
findings.location: Security section ('SSRF-safe JWKS fetch') vs Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC8 (no AC covers HTTPS enforcement, timeout bounding, or private-host rejection)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default JWKS fetch timeout and cache TTL (or explicitly delegate to PyJWKClient's defaults) in the 'What' or Security section, and decide whether they are user-configurable `auth_jwt_*` keys.
findings.finding: The JWKS fetch timeout and JWKS cache TTL are described as 'bounded' in the Security section but no concrete default values are given. The executor must choose these numbers without guidance, which is a defaultable gap.
findings.location: Security section ('HTTPS-only, bounded timeout'; 'JWKS cached with a bounded TTL')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'JWKS fetch timeout: 10 s (not user-configurable). JWKS cache TTL: delegated to `PyJWKClient` built-in (lifespan parameter default); a future story may expose `auth_jwt_jwks_cache_ttl`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the 'regenerated reference docs' sub-claim in AC2 (e.g., `make docs && grep -E 'auth_jwt_jwks_uri' docs/reference/env-vars.md`).
findings.finding: AC2 requires 'regenerated reference docs' but states only `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` as its proving command. There is no explicit proving command for verifying that the reference docs were actually regenerated (a distinct artifact from the manifest).
findings.location: AC2: 'The auth_jwt_* flat keys … appear in the regenerated reference docs, and scripts/check_server_manifest.py passes.'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'Verified by: `make docs && grep -c auth_jwt_jwks_uri docs/reference/env-vars.md` returns ≥ 1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or `CLAUDE.md` / design-doc section) capturing the JWKS-verifier architectural decisions: algorithm-pinning contract, symmetric-alg rejection rule, SSRF-safe JWKS-fetch invariants, composite-audience pattern, and rate-limited rotation — so future verifier implementations inherit the same security posture.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural security pattern (JWKS-backed verifier on the S2 seam, with SSRF mitigations, algorithm-confusion guards, and rate-limited key rotation) but does not call for capturing these decisions in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc — only reference-doc regeneration for config keys is specified.
findings.location: ## What / ## Security / ## Acceptance Criteria AC-2
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-jwks-jwt-verifier-security-posture.md` (or append to an existing security ADR) documenting: (1) asymmetric-only JWKS path rule, (2) aud==resource_server contract from S2, (3) SSRF-safe fetch invariant, (4) rate-limited kid-refetch policy, (5) typ-check rationale. Reference it from the `## Security` section of this plan and link it from CLAUDE.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: specify that JWKS fetch failures, token validation rejections (with reason category), and unknown-kid refetch rate-limit events are logged at WARN/ERROR with enough context (issuer, kid, reason) for operator diagnosis.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation (log lines, metrics, or structured events) for JWKS fetch failures, token validation rejections, or cache hit/miss events. Operators have no signal to distinguish 'JWKS endpoint is down' from 'attacker flooding bad tokens' from 'clock skew causing exp rejections'.
findings.location: Security section / AC6 / (no observability section exists)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the JWKS refetch for an unknown kid is deduplicated under concurrency (e.g., a per-verifier asyncio.Lock or single-flight/promise pattern) so that N concurrent cache-miss requests collapse to a single JWKS fetch, and clarify that the shared `PyJWKClient` instance is accessed under that same lock.
findings.finding: The plan mentions 'rate-limited/cooled-down' refetch for unknown kids but does not address concurrent-request thundering herd: if N async requests simultaneously present tokens with an unknown kid before any refetch completes, N concurrent JWKS fetches may be issued. The plan also does not specify whether the shared `PyJWKClient` cache object is safe for concurrent mutation from multiple threads/coroutines.
findings.location: Security (Key rotation without DoS) / AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Use an `asyncio.Lock` around the refetch path: the first coroutine to see an unknown kid acquires the lock, fetches, and releases; subsequent coroutines wait and then re-check the cache before deciding whether to fetch again. This provides both the single-flight deduplication and the concurrency-safe cache update in one mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` as an edit target and update line 208 to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS` (or a filtered subset) so the new `auth_jwt_*` entries appear in the rendered Gate-vars table in `docs/mcp-reference.md`.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` line 208 hardcodes a static tuple `('REBAR_MCP_READONLY', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC')` for the 'Gate environment variables' rendered table and does NOT iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`. Any new `auth_jwt_*` entries added to `MCP_ENV_VARS` will be verified by `check_server_manifest.py` (in `server.json`) but will be silently absent from the rendered `docs/mcp-reference.md` gate-vars table. The plan says AC2 requires keys to 'appear in the regenerated reference docs' but does not enumerate `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` as an edit target.
findings.location: AC2 / scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py line 208
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the static tuple at line 208 with `for v in MCP_ENV_VARS if not v.get('deprecated', False)` to render all current (non-deprecated) env vars from the single source of truth, so new entries added in S3 (and future stories) are automatically included.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the kid-flood suppression mechanism the implementation must add on top of `PyJWKClient` — e.g., a bounded 'recently-seen-bad-kids' set with a cooldown TTL or a request-count cap per time window — so the developer knows what to build and AC7 ('observable oracle via request count') has a clear implementation target.
findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` does not natively rate-limit or cool-down refetches for unknown `kid` values — it will refetch on every unrecognized `kid`, enabling an attacker-controlled kid-flood to cause unbounded JWKS fetches. The plan mandates this protection as both a security hard requirement and an AC (with observable oracle via request count), but gives no implementation direction for the wrapper logic the developer must add on top of `PyJWKClient`.
findings.location: Security section ('Key rotation without DoS') and AC line 7 ('unknown-`kid` refetch is rate-limited/cooled-down... observable oracle via the stateful JWKS fake's request count')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Because `PyJWKClient` will refetch on every unknown `kid`, the verifier must maintain a short-lived "bad-kid" cooldown set (e.g., a `dict[str, float]` of `kid → last_refetch_timestamp`) with a configurable cooldown (default 30 s); a `kid` in the cooldown set skips the refetch and raises immediately. The stateful JWKS fake in the unit test should assert the HTTP request count stays bounded (≤ N) across a flood of M >> N distinct unknown kids.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
